title: Surge Capacity, Psychosocial Care & Vulnerable Populations
learningObjectives: Define surge capacity and the four S's (staff, stuff, space, systems).
Describe conventional, contingency, and crisis standards of care.
Recognize the psychological impact of disasters on survivors and responders.
Describe psychological first aid and responder resilience measures.
Explain the CMIST framework for at-risk and vulnerable populations.
Integrate these concepts into recovery and continuity planning.

summary.title: Surge Capacity and the Four S's
summary.content: Surge capacity is a health system's ability to rapidly expand beyond normal services to meet a sudden increase in demand. It is built on four pillars — the four S's: Staff (clinical and support personnel, including callback and credentialed volunteers), Stuff (supplies, medications, equipment, PPE), Space (beds, alternate care sites, treatment areas), and Systems (command, communications, tracking, policies). A weakness in any one S caps the whole surge — plenty of beds are useless without staff to work them.

summary.title: Conventional, Contingency, Crisis Standards
summary.content: As demand rises, care moves along a continuum. Conventional care uses normal spaces, staff, and supplies. Contingency care uses adaptations that are functionally equivalent — repurposed spaces, substituted equipment, adjusted staffing ratios. Crisis standards of care are reached when demand overwhelms resources despite all adaptations, requiring a formally declared, ethically and legally framed shift to allocating scarce resources for population benefit. The goal throughout is to stay in conventional/contingency as long as possible and enter crisis standards deliberately, not by drift.

summary.title: The Psychological Toll
summary.content: Disasters injure minds as well as bodies. Survivors may experience acute stress reactions, grief, anxiety, depression, and later post-traumatic stress; the 'worried well' and psychological casualties can outnumber the physically injured, especially after radiological, chemical, or terrorist events. Pre-existing mental illness may decompensate when medications and routines are lost. Behavioral-health needs are part of the casualty count, not an afterthought.

summary.title: Psychological First Aid and Responder Resilience
summary.content: Psychological first aid (PFA) is the evidence-informed early response: ensure safety and basic needs, provide calm and comfort, connect people to loved ones and information, and link them to services — without forcing people to recount trauma. Responders themselves are at risk: cumulative stress, moral distress, and burnout degrade performance and health. Resilience measures include realistic training, work-rest cycles, buddy systems, leadership support, and post-event debriefing and follow-up. The recovery link of the disaster cycle includes caring for the caregivers.

summary.title: Vulnerable Populations and the CMIST Framework
summary.content: Disasters fall hardest on those least able to cope. The CMIST framework organizes access and functional needs across populations rather than by diagnosis: Communication (people with limited English, hearing, or vision needs), Maintaining health (those dependent on medications, oxygen, dialysis, or medical devices/power), Independence (people who rely on mobility aids, assistive devices, or personal-care attendants), Support/Safety/Self-determination (people needing supervision, including some with cognitive or psychiatric conditions, and children), and Transportation (those without independent means to evacuate). Planning to CMIST — not to labels — makes response equitable and effective.

summary.title: Tying It Together: Recovery and Continuity
summary.content: Surge, psychosocial care, and vulnerable-population planning all extend into the recovery phase. Continuity of operations keeps essential services running and restores the rest. Recovery addresses the long tail: rebuilding, chronic-care continuity, behavioral-health services for survivors and responders, and after-action review to feed lessons back into mitigation and preparedness. A response is not over when the last acute casualty is treated — it is over when the community and its responders have recovered.

clinicalPearls: The four S's cap each other: staff, stuff, space, systems — the weakest S limits your surge.
Enter crisis standards of care deliberately and formally, never by accidental drift.
After many disasters the psychological casualties outnumber the physical ones — plan behavioral-health capacity.
Plan to CMIST functional needs, not to diagnostic labels — it captures who actually needs help and how.
redFlags: Surge plans that count beds (space) but ignore staff and systems.
Responders showing burnout, moral distress, or acute stress — performance and safety degrade.
Evacuation plans that strand people dependent on power, dialysis, mobility aids, or transportation.
commonMistakes: Treating behavioral-health needs as optional rather than as part of the casualty load.
Forcing survivors to recount their trauma in the name of 'debriefing' — not part of psychological first aid.
Planning for an 'average' patient and missing the access and functional needs of vulnerable groups.
memoryAids: Surge = four S's: Staff, Stuff, Space, Systems.
CMIST: Communication, Maintaining health, Independence, Support/safety/self-determination, Transportation.
PFA: safety, calm, connectedness, self-efficacy, hope — comfort, don't interrogate.

decisionTrees.title: Escalating Demand and Standards of Care
decisionTrees.content:
Demand rising against capacity
  → Maximize surge across the four S's: Staff, Stuff, Space, Systems
  → Conventional care (normal resources) → if exceeded:
  → Contingency care (functionally equivalent adaptations) → if exceeded:
  → Crisis standards of care: FORMALLY declared; ethical/legal framework; allocate scarce resources for population benefit
  → Throughout: address behavioral health (survivors + responders) and CMIST functional needs
  → De-escalate back toward conventional as resources recover; after-action review

references: NDLSF BDLS v4.0 — Surge Capacity and Standards of Care
NDLSF BDLS v4.0 — Psychosocial Issues and At-Risk/Vulnerable Populations (CMIST)
